1:
hāʾādôn YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = "the Lord (hāʾādôn), the LORD of hosts" — two distinct divine titles. Hāʾādôn (with definite article) emphasizes absolute sovereignty; YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = LORD of armies/heavenly hosts. The combination heightens the authority of the following announcement.

mašʿēn ûmašʿēnâ = "support (mašʿēn, masc.) and staff (mašʿēnâ, fem.)" — two forms of the same root (from šāʿan, to lean upon), one masculine, one feminine, creating a comprehensive "all supports" through gender variation. The double word means every kind of thing that can be relied upon. YHWH is removing from Jerusalem every prop on which the city leans — both physical (bread and water, v. 1b) and social (the leadership catalog of vv. 2-3).
2: The removal catalog begins: gibbôr = warrior/mighty man; ʾîš milḥāmâ = man of war (military professional); šōpēṭ = judge; nāḇî = prophet; qōsēm = diviner (included here alongside legitimate leaders — the catalog is sociological rather than theological; all leadership types disappear); zāqēn = elder. The list spans political, military, judicial, prophetic, and divinatory leadership — the entire social structure that holds a city together.
3: The catalog continues: śar-ḥămiššîm = "captain of fifty" — a military rank (cf. 2 Kgs 1:9-15); nĕśûʾ pānîm = "man of standing/person of distinction" (lit. "one whose face is lifted up" — someone with honor and social status); yôʿēṣ = counselor/advisor; ḥăkam ḥărāšîm = "skilled artisan/master craftsman" (ḥāraš = craftsman, especially metalworker or woodworker); nĕḇôn laḥaš = "expert in charms/enchantments" (lāḥaš = whispered charm, serpent charming). The inclusion of craftsmen and enchanters alongside military captains shows the comprehensive social disintegration: even technical and magical expertise disappears.
4:
wĕnātattî nĕʿārîm śārêhem = "I will make youths (nĕʿārîm) their rulers" — nāʿar = youth, boy (often with connotations of inexperience, rashness, or servile status). The word that in other contexts describes Joseph as a young servant (Gen 39:2) or Rehoboam's bad counselors (1 Kgs 12:8-11) now names the rulers of Jerusalem. The reversal of age and wisdom hierarchies is the judgment.

ûtaʿălûlîm yimšĕlû-ḇām = "and infants/capricious ones will govern them" — taʿălûl (H8586) is rare and disputed: "infants" (NIV/ESV), "capricious ones" (NJPS), "babes" (KJV). The root ʿālaʿ suggests wantonness or playful caprice. Either way: the city that once had Solomonic wisdom will be governed by those who have neither age nor discernment.
5:
wĕniggaś hāʿam ʾîš bĕʾîš = "The people will oppress each other, man against man" — the social contract collapses from horizontal solidarity into mutual exploitation. Nāgaś (H5065) = to press, drive, exacte (the same word used for Egyptian slave-drivers in Exod 3:7; 5:6). Every member of the community becomes a slave-driver to every other.

wĕhazzāqēn = "and the elder" — the reversal of the age hierarchy from v. 4 is now stated socially: youth is "arrogant against (yirgĕzôz)" the elder; the base (niqaleh, from qālal = to be light, contemptible) against the honorable (nikkābēd, from kāḇēḏ = heavy/weighty/honored). These are the honor-shame categories of the ancient world: kāḇēḏ (weighty) = honorable; qālal (light) = contemptible. Social disintegration is expressed through the inversion of these categories.
6:
kî-yiṯpōś ʾîš bĕʾāḥîw = "for a man will seize his own brother" — the seizure (tāpas = to grasp, catch) is coercive: grabbing an unwilling person for forced leadership. The desperation of the scenario: even the brother is the one seized, pointing to the complete breakdown of family solidarity as a social resource.

śalmâ lĕkā qāṣîn tihyeh-lānû = "you have a robe (śalmâ), be our leader/ruler (qāṣîn)!" The only qualification offered for leadership is possession of a garment — the minimum visible marker of social standing above the desperately poor. The depletion of all other social resources has made a robe sufficient credential for authority.
7:
yiśśāʾ bayyôm hahûʾ lēʾmōr = "he will protest on that day, saying" — the naśāʾ qôl (lifting of the voice) is a formal response to the seizure of v. 6. The refusal of leadership is not cowardice but honest assessment: the designated leader has nothing to give.

lōʾ-ʾehyeh ḥōḇēš = "I am no healer/binder of wounds" — ḥōḇēš from ḥāḇaš = to bind up wounds (used in Isa 1:6; also Ezek 30:21). The word connects back to ch. 1's body-politic metaphor: the city's wounds are unbound (1:6), and now no one is willing to be the wound-binder. The social disintegration of ch. 3 is the direct consequence of the moral disintegration of ch. 1.
8:
kî kāšĕlâ yĕrûšālāim wîhûḏâ nāpāl = "for Jerusalem has stumbled (kāšal) and Judah has fallen (nāpal)" — the two verbs describe stages of collapse: stumbling (destabilization) followed by falling (collapse). Jerusalem precedes Judah (the capital before the province) — the failure begins at the center and extends outward.

lĕšônām wĕmaʿalĕlêhem ʾel-YHWH = "their tongue (lāšôn) and their deeds (maʿălāl) against the LORD" — both speech and action are directed against YHWH. lĕmārôt ʿênê kĕḇôdô = "defying/provoking (mārar) the eyes of his glory (kāḇôd)" — the divine kāḇôd (glory) is not merely abstract honor but a visual presence (Isa 6:3); defying "the eyes of his glory" means acting as if YHWH does not see and is not present.
9:
hakarāt pĕnêhem ʿānĕtâ bām = "the expression (hakārat) of their faces has testified against them" — hakkārāh = recognition, appearance; what the face reveals about the person. The moral state of Jerusalem is so manifest that the faces of its people are self-indicting testimony. They do not need to be charged — they accuse themselves.

wĕḥaṭṭāʾtām kĕsĕdôm higgîdû lōʾ kiḥēdû = "they parade (higgîd) their sin like Sodom and do not conceal (kāḥad) it." The Sodom comparison recurs from 1:9-10, but here the charge is specific: deliberate, shameless public display of what Sodom did. The sin is not hidden but flaunted — the opposite of the shame that might lead to repentance.
10: ʾimrû ṣaddîq kî-ṭôḇ = "Say of the righteous that it will go well (ṭôḇ) for him" — the call to proclaim good news to the righteous is a counter-note within the judgment oracle. Even in the midst of social chaos (vv. 1-9), there is a word for the righteous: they will eat the fruit of their actions. Kî pĕrî maʿalĕlêhem yōʾkēlû = "for the fruit of their deeds (maʿălāl) they will eat." The same maʿălāl (deeds) used pejoratively in vv. 8 serves here positively: righteous deeds produce righteous fruit.
11: ʾôy lĕrāšāʿ rāʿ = "Woe to the wicked — it will go badly (rāʿ)" — the second ôy (woe) in the book (cf. 1:4). The lex talionis principle stated: kîggĕmûl yādāyw yēʿāśeh-llô = "for what his hands have done will be done to him in return." Gāmal = to deal out, recompense; the reflexive return of deed to doer. This is the principle underlying both OT and NT eschatology — deeds return to their originator.
12:
ʿammî nōgĕśāyw mĕʿôlēl = "My people — their oppressors are children (mĕʿôlēl)" — mĕʿôlēl is disputed: "infants/children" (LXX) or "those who treat them like children" (i.e., capriciously). The connection to v. 4 (nĕʿārîm and taʿălûlîm) suggests "children" is correct — the judgment of immature rulers is now being experienced.

wĕnāšîm māšĕlû-ḇô = "and women rule over them" — read within its ANE context, not modern: female rule was the cultural marker of a society whose men had been killed, exiled, or disempowered. The point is the inversion of expected leadership structures, not a critique of women per se. mĕʾaššĕrêkā maṭʿîm = "your guides lead you astray" (lit. "those who call you blessed cause you to err") — the very people who provide reassurance are those leading into error.
13: niṣṣāḇ lārîḇ YHWH wĕʿōmēd lādîn ʿammîm = "the LORD has taken his stand (niṣṣāḇ, niphal of yāṣaḇ) to bring a charge (rîb) and stands (ʿōmēd) to judge (dîn) peoples." The rîb (lawsuit) pattern from ch. 1 is explicitly invoked: YHWH as both prosecutor and judge. The prophetic perfect (niphal of yāṣaḇ) treats the future judicial action as already accomplished in its certainty.
14: bĕziqnê ʿammô ûśārāyw yāḇôʾ bamišpāṭ = "He will enter into judgment (mišpāṭ) with the elders and rulers (śārîm) of his people" — specifically the leaders of Israel are the defendants, not nations in general. The charge against them: wĕʾattem biʿartĕm hakkārem = "you have plundered/burned (bāʿar) the vineyard." Hakkārem = "the vineyard" — the vineyard that is Israel (cf. 5:1-7, where the vineyard imagery is developed into a full allegory). The leaders have burned/pillaged what was entrusted to them.
15: mallĕkem tedakkĕʾû ʿammî = "What do you think you are doing, crushing (dākaʾ) my people?" — dākaʾ = to crush, grind to powder (used for grinding grain; also of the crushed Servant in Isa 53:5). The same verb will describe the Servant who is crushed for our sins. Here it describes what the leaders are doing to the people. ûpĕnê ʿăniyyîm tiṭḥānû = "and grinding the faces of the poor into the dust" — the milling metaphor: the poor are grain being ground under the millstone of economic and judicial exploitation.
16: bĕnôt ṣiyyôn = "daughters of Zion" — the women of Jerusalem, addressed in contrast to the bat-tsion of 1:8 (which is the city personified). Here the daughters are the elite women who have absorbed the city's values. bĕgābah ʿênayim = "with haughty/lifted eyes" — gāḇah in eyes = arrogance (cf. 2:11); ûbĕṭāpheph tēlakhnâ = "and prancing/tripping (ṭāpheph) as they walk" — the verb imitates mincing steps; ûbĕraglêhem tĕʿakkasênāh = "and jingling with their feet (ʿākaś)" — ankle bracelets that make a sound as they walk, advertising their presence and wealth.
17: wĕšippaḥ ʾăḏōnāy qodqod bĕnôt ṣiyyôn = "the Lord will afflict the scalps (qodqod) of the daughters of Zion with sores" — šippaḥ = to lay bare, to make scabby; qodqod = the crown of the head, the top. The proud head (v. 16: lifted eyes, styled hair) is specifically targeted. YHWH pitĕhen yĕʿāreh = "the LORD will expose their nakedness/shame (pōt)" — the revelation of nakedness is the ultimate reversal of the elaborate covering described in vv. 18-23.
18:
The catalog of ornaments removed in vv. 18-23 is the most detailed inventory of women's jewelry and clothing in the OT. Its comprehensiveness mirrors the comprehensiveness of the social catalog in vv. 2-3: just as every form of leadership is removed (vv. 2-3), every form of adornment is removed here. The parallel structure suggests the ornaments are a form of social-power display, not merely personal decoration.

ʿăkāsîm = anklets (cf. v. 16); šĕbîsîm = headbands/hairnets; haśśahărōnîm = crescent ornaments/moon-shaped pendants (from sahăr = moon; possibly related to moon-cult jewelry).
19: Continuing: hannĕṭîpôt = eardrops/pendants (from nāṭap̄ = to drip/hang); haššĕʾērôt = bracelets or chains (disputed: "chains" NIV; "bangles" ESV; from root meaning threaded items?); hārĕʿālôt = veils (of a fine, thin material — cf. Song 5:7).
20: happĕʾērîm = turbans/headdresses (pĕʾēr = ornament, glorify; cf. Isa 61:3 where YHWH gives the "crown of beauty" as a messianic gift — here it is removed); haṣṣĕʿādôt = armlets/ankle chains; haqqiššurîm = sashes/cinctures; bāttê hanneppeš = "houses of breath" = perfume bottles (lit. "houses of the soul/breath" — containers that hold aromatic spirits); haḥăḇāšîm = lucky charms/amulets (lit. "binding items").
21: haṭṭabbāʿôt = signet rings (ṭabbāʿat from ṭāḇaʿ = to dip/seal); signet rings bore the family insignia and were used to seal documents and transactions — both jewelry and instruments of social power and legal identity; nizmê hāʾāp̄ = nose rings/nose ornaments.
22: The catalog transitions to clothing: hammaḥălāṣôt = "holiday garments/festive robes" (from ḥālaṣ = to draw out, strip off — perhaps garments that were put on for festivals then removed); hammĕʿāṭāpôt = "capes/outer robes" (from ʿāṭap̄ = to wrap, cover); hammiṭpāḥôt = "wraps/shawls"; haḥārīṭîm = "purses/bags" (possibly the reticulated bags or purses carried by women of status).
23: haggîlyōnîm = "polished mirrors" (from gālâ = to uncover/reveal; mirrors that reveal appearance); hassĕdînîm = "linen garments/tunics" (fine linen underwear or outer garments); happenîpôt = "tiaras/turbans" (of a specific ceremonial type); harĕḏîdîm = "full-length veils/large wraps" (cf. Song 5:7). The exhaustive catalog concludes here — twenty-one items total, covering head to foot. The rhetorical effect is comprehensive: everything that adorned, defined, and signaled elite status is taken.
24:
wĕhāyâ taḥat-bōśem môqâ = "Instead of sweet perfume (bōśem), there will be a stench (môqâ)" — the four-fold taḥat ("instead of") refrain structures the reversals: perfume → stench; sash → rope; styled hair → shaved head; fine robe → sackcloth (with branded/scorched skin). Taḥat (under/instead of) is the word of covenant curse (Deut 28 uses it repeatedly for reversals of blessing into curse).

kî taḥat yōpî = "for instead of beauty (yōpî)" — the final and climactic reversal: beauty itself, the thing the entire catalog describes, becomes something else. The MKT's "instead of fine robe, a garment of grief (māḥăgōret śaq)" and "burning instead of beauty" captures the complete overturning.
25: mĕtayik bĕḥereḇ yippōlû = "Your men (mĕtê, pl. of met = mortal man) will fall by the sword (ḥereḇ)" — from adornment to battlefield. Wĕgĕḇûrātēk = "your warriors (gĕḇûrâ = strength/might, used for military prowess) in battle (milḥāmâ)." The military capacity that Judah trusted in (2:7: horses, chariots) will be destroyed in battle.
26: wĕʾāněllû wĕʾāḇĕlû pĕtāḥeyhā = "And her city gates will mourn (ʾānal) and grieve (ʾāḇal)" — the personification of Jerusalem as a woman continues from 1:8 (bat-tsion); now the city gates (the places of commerce, judgment, and social life) mourn. Pĕtāḥîm = gate-openings. wĕnuqqĕtâ lāʾāreṣ tēšēḇ = "she will sit desolate (nāqāh = emptied, stripped) on the ground" — sitting on the ground is the posture of mourning (Job 2:13; Lam 2:10; Ezek 26:16). Jerusalem, stripped of its adornments (vv. 16-23) and its men (v. 25), sits in mourning on the ground.
